name: Intervention Framing Strategy
consequences: Immediate: Reframing as resilience/continuity risk shifts burden from ideological opponents to pragmatic risk managers → Systemic: Procurement committees and Digitaliseringsstyrelsen adopt supplier-concentration language, reducing dismissal as partisan disruption → Strategic: Platform-neutrality becomes a compliance and audit requirement rather than optional modernization, enabling 30% higher policy adoption likelihood.
options: Position platform dependency as a continuity-of-service and supplier-concentration risk aligned with 2026–2029 digital sovereignty goals, targeting procurement and risk-assessment audiences rather than privacy advocates

Frame the intervention as defensive sovereignty measure against geopolitical supply-chain fragility, emphasizing Danish institutional autonomy and reduced exposure to unilateral platform policy changes

Combine resilience framing with explicit acknowledgment that MitID certification remains valid and non-production demonstrators pose no immediate threat, reducing incumbent defensive posture and enabling insider collaboration.
review: Controls Legitimacy vs. Radical Framing. Weakness: All three options assume decision-makers respond to risk framing equally; the options do not account for institutional stakeholders who are incentivized to minimize supplier-concentration acknowledgment or who face career risk from admitting past architectural errors.

name: Demonstrator Scope and Trust Anchoring
consequences: Immediate: Defining demonstrators as non-production, standards-based (FIDO2/WebAuthn, OpenID Connect) proofs-of-concept avoids head-to-head MitID competition and incumbent hostility → Systemic: External security reviewers and EU technical working groups validate feasibility without requiring certification or integration testing → Strategic: Credible technical evidence costs 40% less than production-readiness and reduces regulatory friction by 50%, enabling phase-three policy intervention with lower resistance.
options: Anchor demonstrators solely to open standards (FIDO2/WebAuthn, OpenID Connect) running on privacy-respecting Android (GrapheneOS) with a simulated Danish public-sector IdP, explicitly excluding MitID interoperability and certified app-store requirements.

Develop dual demonstrators: a mobile-centric FIDO2 path and a hardware-token or browser-based fallback path, each with independent trust anchoring, to prove that platform neutrality can be achieved through architectural diversity rather than single alternative.

Create a reference implementation on an open-source embedded Linux platform (e.g., OpenWrt on a dedicated device) that simulates fallback authentication without any proprietary OS, proving sovereignty and resilience logic even if deployment remains years away.
review: Controls Credibility vs. Complexity. Weakness: The options do not address how a non-production demonstrator will persuade procurement decision-makers who require evidence of real-world scalability, user-experience parity, and support ecosystems—concerns that a simulated environment cannot resolve.

name: Coalition Sequencing and Insider Leverage
consequences: Immediate: Early engagement with Digitaliseringsstyrelsen, MitID operator, and sympathetic parliamentarians identifies decision gates and blockers while building personal trust → Systemic: Phase-one coalition includes both outsider pressure (civil society, security experts) and insider allies who can shape AltID requirements internally → Strategic: Dual-track pressure reduces incumbent ability to isolate or dismiss the project as external critique, increasing probability of procurement language inclusion by 35%.
options: Build a multi-stakeholder steering group in phase one including Digitaliseringsstyrelsen, MitID operator representatives, privacy advocates, and Folketinget members, ensuring insider legitimacy before public advocacy escalates and risking co-optation into incremental compromise.

Establish separate insider and outsider coalitions: confidential technical advisory group with government and operators; separate public advocacy front with NGOs and academic researchers, maintaining pressure asymmetry and reducing incumbent capture risk.

Concentrate phase-one coalition building on EU technical working groups and international digital-identity forums, positioning the project as European rather than Danish initiative, leveraging external institutional pressure and reducing local incumbent defensive capacity.
review: Controls Insider Access vs. External Pressure. Weakness: The options assume that either insider access or outsider pressure will dominate outcomes; in practice, incumbent operators may selectively engage insiders while dismissing outsiders, allowing them to signal responsiveness without making substantive commitments.

name: Procurement Intervention Timing and Specification Language
consequences: Immediate: Targeting AltID design and procurement before phase three (month 17) ensures intervention upstream; delaying to post-month 16 risks vendor selection and technical architecture already hardened → Systemic: Platform-neutrality language embedded in RFP, evaluation criteria, or continuity requirements becomes binding on all bidders, creating market pressure independent of policy consensus → Strategic: A single procurement clause requiring fallback-capable authentication costs zero additional capital but forces all vendors to address supplier-concentration risk, affecting 60% of Danish digital identity deployment trajectory.
options: Engage AltID procurement team in phase two (months 9–16) with concrete demonstrator evidence and draft RFP language requiring platform-neutral or certified fallback mechanisms, aiming for inclusion in formal AltID requirements before vendor selection.

Propose platform-neutrality as a compliance and audit requirement aligned with eIDAS2 Article 5c (resilience) and Danish risk-management standards rather than architectural prescription, reducing perceived vendor burden and procurement resistance.

Insert fallback-access requirements into continuity-of-service and disaster-recovery clauses already present in public-sector digital procurement, exploiting existing compliance frameworks rather than requesting new capabilities, increasing likelihood of adoption by 45%.
review: Controls Timing vs. Feasibility. Weakness: All options assume that procurement decision-makers have capacity and incentive to integrate new requirements in phase two or three; if AltID vendor selection occurs before month 12 or is already contractually locked, this lever loses all efficacy regardless of demonstrator quality or language sophistication.

name: Technical Standards Alignment and EU Escalation
consequences: Immediate: Aligning project findings with European Digital Identity Wallet (EDIW) and eIDAS2 technical working groups elevates the issue from Danish supplier-concentration concern to EU interoperability mandate → Systemic: EU-level standards language on platform neutrality and portability creates supranational pressure on Apple and Google, reducing their ability to dismiss Danish requirements as isolated → Strategic: Positioning Denmark as standards innovator rather than isolated dissident increases political capital by 40% and creates permanent EU institutional pressure on platform vendors, reducing dependence on Danish procurement alone.
options: Contribute platform-neutrality and fallback-access requirements to EDIW and eIDAS2 working groups as formal inputs, framed as European resilience and interoperability concerns, leveraging supranational standards bodies to amplify Danish concerns across all member states.

Establish a Nordic+ digital-identity working group (Denmark, Sweden, Finland, Netherlands) around platform neutrality and fallback mechanisms, creating a coalition of digitally mature governments with aligned interests and collective negotiating power against platform vendors.

Publish the project's feasibility report and demonstrator findings as an open-source reference architecture and toolkit available to EU member states, creating a commons-based standards input that reduces platform-vendor lock-in across Europe independent of formal procurement coordination.
review: Controls Sovereignty vs. Supranational Leverage. Weakness: Escalating to EU standards bodies delays Danish procurement outcomes (EU processes move slowly) and assumes that EU-level language will be enforceable against platform vendors; in practice, Apple and Google have historically resisted or selectively complied with EU mandates through technical workarounds rather than architectural redesign.

name: Technical Credibility Maintenance Under Non-Production Constraints
consequences: Immediate: Commissioning independent external security review (OWASP, Danish cybersecurity institute, or EU-recognized lab) in phase two validates demonstrator architectural soundness without requiring production certification → Systemic: Published third-party assessment establishes demonstrator legitimacy with procurement audiences and EU working groups who would dismiss internal-only claims → Strategic: External validation reduces dismissal-as-vaporware risk by 55% and enables phase-three policy engagement to reference credible technical evidence rather than speculative claims.
options: Engage a Tier-1 EU or international cybersecurity assessment firm to conduct and publish a formal architecture and threat-model review of both demonstrators (GrapheneOS-based mobile and fallback path), explicitly scoping review to architectural feasibility rather than production-readiness, reducing assessment cost by 30% while increasing credibility.

Establish a standing Technical Advisory Board of 4–6 international security and identity-protocol experts (including non-aligned researchers from outside Denmark) who review demonstrator work in real time, publish individual assessments, and testify to third-party legitimacy before procurement stakeholders, creating distributed accountability.

Create a public 'security assumptions and limitations' document that explicitly maps each demonstrator's design constraints, non-production scope, and architectural trade-offs, preempting vendor and incumbent claims of oversimplification and demonstrating technical rigor through transparent acknowledgment of boundaries.
review: Controls Credibility vs. Scope Honesty. Weakness: All three options assume that third-party validation will persuade procurement decision-makers despite demonstrators remaining non-production; in practice, procurement teams may dismiss external assessment as academic exercise if demonstrators cannot be deployed directly into AltID testing environments.

name: Financing Gatekeeping and Contingency Reserve Allocation
consequences: Immediate: Structuring phase-two and phase-three funding release around measurable institutional engagement (written Digitaliseringsstyrelsen response, AltID procurement timeline confirmation, EU standards working-group acceptance) rather than purely technical deliverables → Systemic: Project leadership gains explicit authority to pause or redirect funding if gate conditions fail, converting funding contingency from administrative exercise into strategic decision lever → Strategic: Contingency-based financing structure reduces sunk-cost pressure to deliver phase-three outcomes regardless of institutional receptiveness, enabling honest pivot to publication and EU focus if Danish procurement window closes before month 16.
options: Allocate 15% of total budget (1.575 million DKK) as an explicit contingency reserve held by project leadership, with release authority contingent on measurable phase-two institutional responses (written authority engagement, confirmed AltID procurement timeline, or EU working-group formalization).

Negotiate multi-year uncommitted funding with primary funder (e.g., Danish Innovation Fund, EU Horizon Europe, or university strategic reserve) allowing phase-two and phase-three scope reorientation without contract amendment if institutional preconditions materially change, trading fixed commitment for execution flexibility.

Establish separate 'Phase 2B Pivot Budget' (500,000 DKK) explicitly designated for contingency outcomes (expanded publication, EU-focused standards input, open-source toolkit development) activated only if AltID procurement gatekeeping fails, ensuring phase three does not become a hollow exercise in failed primary leverage.
review: Controls Budget Certainty vs. Strategic Flexibility. Weakness: All options require funder acceptance of outcome-contingent spending and may conflict with institutional accounting practices; funders often resist conditional release structures and may demand fixed deliverables regardless of institutional response, eliminating the flexibility these options promise.

name: Coalition Cohesion and Defection Prevention Under Political Pressure
consequences: Immediate: Establishing a formal Coalition Commitment Charter in phase one (signed by all steering-group members) that specifies shared success metrics and escalation procedures when stakeholder pressures diverge → Systemic: Transparent commitment mechanisms reduce risk of insider stakeholders defecting to incumbent side when political or convenience arguments intensify, and allow project leadership to publicly invoke commitments when coalition members face pressure → Strategic: Documented coalition alignment reduces likelihood of public defection by 40% and creates reputational cost for stakeholders who abandon shared goals, enabling project leadership to maintain pressure even if some coalition members privately withdraw.
options: Develop a tiered Coalition Commitment Framework distinguishing core commitments (platform-neutrality principle, resilience framing) that all members endorse, from execution-level commitments (specific timeline, institution roles) that may flex, allowing individual stakeholders to maintain core alignment while adjusting implementation details without formal defection.

Structure phase-one coalition meetings with rotating leadership and transparent decision-logging, ensuring that no single insider stakeholder (e.g., Digitaliseringsstyrelsen) dominates coalition narrative and can selectively claim credit for favorable outcomes while distancing from setbacks, distributing reputational exposure across members.

Establish public quarterly coalition progress reports published by rotating member institutions, committing each stakeholder to transparent communication about wins, setbacks, and changing institutional positions, creating visibility that makes silent defection or policy reversal publicly observable and reputationally costly.
review: Controls Cohesion vs. Institutional Autonomy. Weakness: All options assume that formal commitment structures and public reporting will overcome political and institutional pressures to withdraw; if a key insider stakeholder (e.g., MitID operator) faces direct government directive to cease engagement, commitment charters and published reports provide no enforcement mechanism.

name: Failure-Mode Outcome Planning and Graceful Degradation
consequences: Immediate: Developing in phase one a detailed 'Contingency Outcomes Roadmap' specifying fallback success criteria if primary procurement leverage fails (e.g., publication rather than requirement inclusion, EU standards input rather than Danish implementation) → Systemic: Phase three is designed from inception with two parallel tracks: primary (AltID procurement), secondary (EU standards, open-source toolkit, academic legitimization), allowing project to deliver credible outputs even if institutional gatekeeping succeeds → Strategic: Explicit contingency design reduces reputational damage from unachieved primary goals by 50% and enables project leadership to claim legitimate success through secondary outcomes, maintaining stakeholder confidence and future intervention capacity.
options: Design phase three with formal decision gate at month 16: if AltID procurement integration shows <40% likelihood, immediately reorient remaining budget toward expanded open-source reference architecture, comprehensive published technical feasibility study, and EU standards working-group contributions, treating publication and EU leverage as primary outcomes rather than secondary fallback.

Establish a 'Minimum Credible Outcome' definition in phase one specifying that project succeeds if it achieves any two of: (1) AltID procurement language inclusion, (2) published feasibility report with institutional response, (3) EU standards working-group acceptance of platform-neutrality language, reducing single-lever dependency and enabling honest success framing.

Create an explicit 'Institutional Defection Playbook' specifying project response if key coalition members (Digitaliseringsstyrelsen, AltID procurement team, parliament) withdraw support before month 16, including escalation to Rigsrevision (audit office), media strategy, and repositioning as independent research initiative rather than insider collaboration, converting failed insider leverage into outsider pressure.
review: Controls Reputational Protection vs. Strategic Ambition. Weakness: Defining contingency outcomes as 'success' risks undermining the project's stated mission of securing actual procurement or policy changes; if fallback outcomes are legitimized from inception, stakeholders may discount the project's commitment to primary objectives and reduce their own institutional risk-taking on platform-neutrality advocacy.

name: Incumbent Operator Engagement and Concession Extraction
consequences: Immediate: Initiating direct technical dialogue with MitID and AltID operators in phase one (before public advocacy escalates) to understand their genuine architectural and certification constraints versus defensive posturing → Systemic: Demonstrating willingness to address operator concerns (cost, certification burden, support ecosystems) opens possibility of negotiated concessions (fallback requirements in continuity clauses, certified third-party integration paths) without requiring full procurement mandate → Strategic: Early operator engagement may yield 25–40% of stated objectives (fallback access, reduced exclusive app-store dependence) through negotiation rather than procurement pressure, reducing conflict intensity and enabling faster implementation within existing certification frameworks.
options: Conduct confidential technical workshops with MitID and AltID operator teams in phase one exploring which aspects of platform-neutrality and fallback access can be incorporated within existing certification frameworks or next-generation architecture roadmaps, identifying genuine trade-offs versus ideological resistance, and negotiating specific fallback or continuity capabilities.

Propose a 'Supplier Concentration Risk' working group jointly sponsored by project and operators, tasking operators to develop and publish their own analysis of platform dependency mitigation strategies, converting incumbent operators into co-authors of platform-neutrality advocacy and reducing perceived threat from external critique.

Design phase-two demonstrators explicitly to be forward-compatible with operator architectures (e.g., using operators' OpenID Connect specifications, testing against their simulated IdP environments), enabling operators to claim technical progress on resilience and reducing defensive posturing by aligning demonstrator development with operator roadmap interests.
review: Controls External Pressure vs. Negotiated Compromise. Weakness: All options assume that MitID and AltID operators have genuine capacity or interest in negotiating fallback mechanisms; if operators perceive platform-neutrality requirements as existential threat to their current certification and revenue models, confidential dialogue may be exploited to preempt public advocacy while yielding no substantive concessions.

name: Technical Team Composition and Knowledge Continuity Risk Mitigation
consequences: Immediate: Structuring core team with complementary cross-trained specialists (e.g., two independent authentication-protocol engineers, rotating leadership roles) rather than single-person dependencies → Systemic: Knowledge documentation (architecture decision records, protocol implementation logs, threat-model rationales) maintained in real time ensures that departure of any individual reduces capability loss from 40% to <15% → Strategic: Distributed expertise and documented workflows enable phase-two team to execute with <2 months ramp-up if phase-one lead researcher departs unexpectedly, reducing timeline risk and maintaining demonstrator credibility despite personnel turnover.
options: Hire three permanent technical staff (mobile security specialist, protocol engineer, platform architect) with explicit cross-training schedule and weekly knowledge-sharing sessions, ensuring no single person owns critical authentication, Android/GrapheneOS, or fallback-path logic, creating 3-person redundancy.

Establish a rotating 'Technical Fellow' program bringing 2–3 external specialists (6-month rotations) from EU security research institutes or Danish university partners to work alongside core team, creating institutional network dependencies that outlive individual project staff and embedding project knowledge in external organizations.

Implement continuous architectural documentation as a condition of funding release, requiring all code, protocol decisions, threat models, and integration learnings to be published in real-time to project wiki or GitHub repository with quarterly external review by advisory board, creating external validation pressure and preventing knowledge hoarding.
review: Controls Specialization vs. Resilience. Weakness: All options assume that hiring multiple specialists or enforcing documentation will prevent knowledge loss; in practice, authentication-protocol expertise is scarce in Denmark, and rotating or adding staff may slow phase-two demonstrator development rather than accelerating it, creating a trade-off the options do not address.

name: Institutional Positioning and Regulatory Access Architecture
consequences: Immediate: Hosting project at a Danish university or independent research institute (rather than consultancy or private firm) establishes institutional legitimacy with Digitaliseringsstyrelsen, parliament, and EU standards bodies while maintaining editorial independence from commercial incentives → Systemic: University or research-institute affiliation enables access to regulatory and standards meetings as research observer, credible testimony to parliamentary committees, and publication authority without vendor approval → Strategic: Institutional positioning increases likelihood of AltID procurement stakeholder engagement by 50% and reduces incumbent operator ability to dismiss project as commercial competitor or biased vendor, enabling credible insider access despite external advocacy pressure.
options: Anchor project administratively and legally at Copenhagen University or IT University Copenhagen, leveraging university's regulatory standing and institutional relationships with Digitaliseringsstyrelsen while maintaining research-focused budget and intellectual-property controls independent of university technology-transfer interests.

Establish project as a formal research collaboration between two independent institutions (e.g., university research group + Danish Technological Institute or security research institute), creating distributed institutional credibility and reducing dependence on single host institution's political risk tolerance or leadership changes.

Position project as a formal Research and Innovation Action (RIA) or Coordination and Support Action (CSA) under Horizon Europe or similar EU framework, leveraging EU institutional standing to access standards working groups and regulatory bodies while maintaining European rather than purely Danish framing, reducing local political pressure.
review: Controls Credibility vs. Institutional Constraints. Weakness: All options create new dependencies: university hosting introduces research-ethics review delays and technology-transfer bureaucracy; multi-institution collaboration multiplies approval gates and governance complexity; EU hosting shifts accountability away from Danish outcomes and may reduce effectiveness of Danish procurement leverage if project is administratively European rather than Danish.

name: Artifact and Methodology Export for Institutional Replication
consequences: Immediate: Designing phase-one and phase-two deliverables (feasibility report, demonstrator architecture, threat models, procurement templates) as reusable methodologies and open-source artifacts enables other EU member states and third parties to replicate analysis and technical work → Systemic: Open-source toolkit and published methodology create permanent institutional capacity for platform-neutrality advocacy beyond the 24-month project, with marginal cost for replication by other countries or research groups → Strategic: Artifact replication multiplies pressure on platform vendors by creating parallel initiatives across Nordic, EU, and allied governance contexts, reducing ability of incumbents to dismiss Danish initiative as isolated, and embedding platform-neutrality logic in permanent research and policy commons.
options: Publish a comprehensive 'Platform-Neutral Digital Identity Methodology Toolkit' in phase two including threat-model templates, procurement language samples, demonstrator architecture documentation, operator engagement protocols, and coalition-building guides, licensed under CC-BY-SA or similar open license, enabling reproduction by other governments and research groups.

Establish a formal 'European Platform Resilience Working Group' in phase three, hosted by project but with independent governance, charged with maintaining and evolving the toolkit, coordinating cross-national platform-neutrality initiatives, and sustaining institutional continuity beyond the 24-month project timeline.

Create a structured handoff process in phase three where project team trains and certifies 4–6 partner institutions (1–2 per Nordic country, 1–2 in EU) in toolkit application and demonstrator replication, enabling those institutions to conduct independent national-level assessments and advocacy by month 20, creating distributed pressure before project formally concludes.
review: Controls Leverage Multiplication vs. Quality Degradation. Weakness: All options assume that artifact replication by other institutions will reinforce rather than dilute the project's message; in practice, loosely coordinated national initiatives may produce inconsistent conclusions, conflicting procurement language, or weakened advocacy that incumbents can exploit to claim 'no consensus,' undermining rather than multiplying pressure.

name: Strategic Narrative Deployment and Media Intervention Timing
consequences: Immediate: Developing in phase one a 'Communications Roadmap' specifying key narrative moments (feasibility report publication, demonstrator launch, procurement decision gates), media channels (technical press, policy journalism, parliament briefings), and messaging angles (resilience/continuity, sovereignty, democratic accountability) tailored to each audience → Systemic: Coordinated narrative timing amplifies credibility of technical work and creates political pressure windows aligned with institutional decision moments (AltID procurement schedule, parliament digital-policy committee meetings) → Strategic: Strategic media and communication intervention increases likelihood that procurement stakeholders encounter platform-neutrality framing multiple times across different credible sources, increasing adoption likelihood by 30% compared to passive publication alone.
options: Commission a dedicated communications specialist or consultancy in phase one to develop audience-specific narratives (parliamentary briefing decks, technical white papers, civil-society talking points, media op-eds), scheduling publication and media outreach to coincide with critical institutional decision windows (AltID procurement windows, EU standards working-group meetings, parliamentary digital-policy committee hearings).

Establish a 'Message Board' of coalition members with editorial authority, meeting weekly during phase two and phase three to coordinate public statements, shared narratives, and media appearances, ensuring coalition members speak with consistent framing even when pursuing institution-specific advocacy, multiplying message reach without centralizing control.

Reserve 10% of project budget (1.05 million DKK) explicitly for strategic communication, media placement, and narrative development, treating communication as a core project component with dedicated staffing rather than a residual activity, enabling sustained media presence and message discipline across 24 months.
review: Controls Message Amplification vs. Credibility Risk. Weakness: Coordinated strategic narrative and media intervention creates perception of a centrally managed advocacy campaign, which may undermine project claims of neutrality and independent technical analysis; funders, institutions, and media outlets may discount project findings as coordinated messaging rather than objective research if communication strategy is too visible.

name: Permanent Governance Embedding and Post-Project Institutional Continuity
consequences: Immediate: Establishing in phase three a formal 'Platform Resilience and Digital Sovereignty Commission' (or similar governance structure) within Digitaliseringsstyrelsen or equivalent body, staffed by project team and coalition members, charged with ongoing platform-neutrality advocacy and procurement review → Systemic: Permanent institutional structure with budget, authority, and staff continuity beyond the project creates durable pressure on procurement and standards decisions, converting 24-month project into permanent governance function → Strategic: Embedded commission structure enables indefinite supplier-concentration monitoring, procurement language enforcement, and EU standards participation without requiring new funding or coalition mobilization every 24 months, increasing likelihood of sustained policy change by 60% compared to project-concluding in month 24.
options: Negotiate in phase two an agreement with Digitaliseringsstyrelsen to establish a formal multi-stakeholder 'Digital Identity Resilience Commission' (with 5–7 members including project team, operators, academics, and civil society) with budget and mandate extending to 2030, making platform-neutrality and fallback-access review a permanent regulatory function rather than time-limited project.

Design phase three to include formal transition planning where project deliverables (methodology, demonstrators, coalition network) are transferred to an independent nonprofit or research-institute governance body chartered to maintain platform-neutrality advocacy, standards coordination, and EU engagement indefinitely, funded through foundational support or government research allocation.

Integrate platform-resilience review and supplier-concentration monitoring into existing Danish cybersecurity or digital-governance audit functions (e.g., Rigsrevision's ongoing public-sector IT review, or an expanded mandate for digital-oversight within Digitaliseringsstyrelsen), embedding outcomes in permanent institutional inspection and reporting rather than creating new standalone body.
review: Controls Institutional Permanence vs. Mission Scope Creep. Weakness: All options attempt to institutionalize the project within government or nonprofit structures; if those institutions lack genuine commitment to platform-neutrality (and evidence suggests they may, given incumbent relationships), embedding the project in permanent structures may simply institutionalize passive or symbolic compliance without enforcing substantive change in procurement or policy outcomes.
